name: LinkedIn API
what_it_does: LinkedIn's REST API for reading profile data, posting content to LinkedIn feeds, managing company pages, and accessing marketing/recruiting tools for approved partners.
use_cases: Posting content to LinkedIn company pages from a content scheduling agent
Reading authenticated user's profile data for personalization or verification
Pulling analytics data from LinkedIn company pages for reporting dashboards
Managing LinkedIn marketing campaigns via the Marketing Developer Platform
Retrieving job postings and application data for recruitment automation (partner-only)
not_for: Reading other users' profiles or connections without partnership approval — this is blocked by default
Building LinkedIn automation bots (liking, connecting, messaging) — aggressively detected and banned
General social listening or public feed monitoring — no public content search API for standard developers
Competitive intelligence scraping — ToS prohibits this and anti-bot systems are sophisticated
Personal account automation — nearly all personal-context write actions require re-auth on each action
best_when: A verified LinkedIn Marketing Partner or approved integration partner needs to post company page content, manage ad campaigns, or pull analytics at scale.
avoid_when: You are a standard developer without LinkedIn program approval — most useful endpoints require partnership status that takes months to obtain and may be denied.

alternatives.reason: More endpoints available on standard developer access, though also expensive
Open protocol for professional announcements without partnership requirements
Simpler Meta-based social API with less restrictive access controls

auth.notes: OAuth 2.0 3-legged auth only for user-context actions — no app-only auth that can write. Access tokens expire in 60 days; refresh tokens valid for 1 year. Re-authorization requires user browser interaction. Scopes include r_liteprofile, r_emailaddress, w_member_social, r_organization_social, w_organization_social. Many scopes require explicit LinkedIn program approval before they can be requested. The 3-legged flow is fundamentally hostile to headless agents.

pricing.free_tier_limits: Basic profile read (r_liteprofile, r_emailaddress) only; Share API for posting (w_member_social) available after approval; company page APIs require Marketing Developer Platform membership
pricing.paid_tiers: Marketing Developer Platform: free to apply but requires LinkedIn approval; Recruiting API requires Talent Solutions partnership; no public pricing for most advanced tiers
pricing.notes: Cost is measured in time and approval friction rather than dollars. Basic access is free but minimal. Most useful capabilities require program membership that involves application, review, compliance audits, and ongoing relationship with LinkedIn. Many applications are rejected without explanation.

performance.rate_limits: Application-level: 100 requests/day for most free endpoints; Member-level: varies; Marketing API: higher limits for approved partners; rate limit documentation is sparse and often inaccurate

agent_readiness.error_message_notes: Error responses are inconsistent — some endpoints return structured JSON errors with codes, others return generic HTTP 403 with no body. The distinction between 'endpoint requires program approval' and 'invalid auth' is often unclear from error messages alone. Debugging access issues requires support tickets.
agent_readiness.idempotency_notes: POST to /v2/shares and /v2/ugcPosts creates duplicate posts if retried. No idempotency key support. Agents must track posted content IDs externally.
known_agent_gotchas: Most interesting endpoints require LinkedIn program membership — standard app registration gives almost no useful write access
3-legged OAuth means every user action requires that specific user to have gone through a browser-based auth flow; fully headless posting is not possible for user-context actions
Access tokens are valid for 60 days but LinkedIn's anti-automation systems may revoke tokens earlier for suspicious activity patterns
Rate limits are poorly documented and change without notice; many developers discover limits only by hitting 429 errors
LinkedIn actively detects and bans automated behavior — unusual posting cadence, bulk connection requests, or scripted interactions trigger account restriction
Company page posting requires both the w_organization_social scope AND the authenticated user to be an admin of that page
API versioning moved to date-based versions (e.g., /rest/posts?linkedin-version=202304) — version pinning is required or behavior can change without notice
No public search API — you cannot search for posts, people, or companies without a specific partnership program

security_score_components.security_notes: TLS enforced on all endpoints. OAuth 2.0 provides reasonable scope separation, but many scopes require program approval making fine-grained access control dependent on LinkedIn's discretion. No service account or app-only auth model for write actions — all write operations require a real user's delegated access. Token storage and rotation is the integrator's responsibility.
